topic: Cursor Composer 2 Guide 2026: Frontier Coding Model at $0.50/M Tokens

competitors.title: Cursor Composer 2 Review 2026: Cursor's First In-House AI Model Takes On Claude Code and OpenAI
key_points: Composer 2 is Anysphere's first proprietary AI model, released March 19, 2026, trained end-to-end for autonomous project-scale coding
Scores 61.3 CursorBench, 61.7 Terminal-Bench 2.0, 73.7 SWE-bench Multilingual — beats Claude Opus 4.6 on Terminal-Bench
38% improvement over Composer 1.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.0, 12% jump on SWE-bench Multilingual
Pricing: Standard $0.50/M input, $2.50/M output; Fast $1.50/M input, $7.50/M output (default)
GPT-5.4 still leads Terminal-Bench 2.0 at 75.1, Claude Opus 4.5 leads SWE-bench Verified at 80.9%
Built on continued pretraining + reinforcement learning (first time Cursor did pretraining before RL)
Deep IDE integration with semantic search, file edits, shell commands, browser control — only frontier model built for IDE-native workflow
200K token context window, compaction-in-the-loop training for long-horizon tasks
Kimi K2.5 controversy: model built on Moonshot AI's open-source base (~25% of computational foundation)
45% of AI-generated code contains security vulnerabilities; 72% security failure rate for AI-generated Java
Self-driving codebase research mode (announced Feb 5, 2026) powers autonomous multi-agent loops
Automations system (March 5, 2026) adds trigger-based agents that run on events like PRs or failing tests

competitors.title: Cursor Composer 2: Benchmarks, Pricing & Review (2026)
key_points: Composer 2 built on Kimi K2.5 (Moonshot AI open-source model) with Cursor's continued pretraining and RL layered on top
Lee Robinson (VP Developer Education) acknowledged ~25% of computational foundation from Kimi K2.5 architecture
MoE (Mixture-of-Experts) architecture — only subset of parameters activates per input, keeps inference fast
Compaction-in-the-loop RL reduces memory errors by 50%, compresses context from 5000+ to ~1000 tokens
86% cheaper than Composer 1.5 (was $3.50/M input, $17.50/M output; now $0.50/$2.50)
Cache-read pricing: $0.20/M standard, $0.35/M fast (vs $0.35/M for Composer 1.5)
Cursor valued at $29.3 billion; Composer 2 launched same day Bloomberg reported $50B valuation talks
Claude Code leads as most-loved AI coding tool at 46% (DataCamp survey), Cursor second at 19%
Individual plans: Composer usage in dedicated pool with generous allocation; Auto mode unlimited on paid plans
Kimi K2.5 controversy: user discovered model ID in API headers, Cursor co-founder called non-disclosure 'a miss'

competitors.title: Cursor Composer 2 AI Coding Model 101
key_points: Third-generation in-house agentic model — specifically tuned for tool use, file edits, terminal operations inside Cursor IDE
Self-summarization training: compresses own memory to ~1000 tokens, 50% reduction in compaction errors
Long-horizon task execution: handles workflows requiring hundreds of individual actions without losing thread
Direct file edits, terminal operations, context-aware code search — doesn't just suggest code, applies diffs
Pricing: 86% cheaper than predecessor; Standard $0.50/M input, Fast $1.50/M input
Cursor plans: Hobby (free, limited), Pro ($20/mo), Pro+ ($60/mo), Teams ($40/user/mo), Enterprise (custom)
GPT-5.4 leads Terminal-Bench 2.0 at 75.1, but Composer 2 is ~5x cheaper on input tokens
SOC 2 certified, supports privacy mode — enterprise-ready compliance
Three Composer generations in 5 months: v1 (Oct 2025), v1.5 (Feb 2026), v2 (Mar 2026)

competitors.title: Cursor Composer 2 Review 2026: Benchmarks & Pricing
key_points: CursorBench 61.3 (+38.7% from v1.5), Terminal-Bench 2.0 61.7 (+28.8%), SWE-bench Multilingual 73.7 (+11.8%)
Two variants: Standard ($0.50/M input, $2.50/M output) and Fast ($1.50/M input, $7.50/M output, default)
Self-summarization already present in v1.5 but significantly improved in v2 with compaction-in-the-loop RL
Continued pretraining on high-quality code + RL for long-horizon tasks, not just fine-tuning a general model
Deep IDE integration: semantic search, file/folder navigation, shell execution, browser control, version control
Only frontier coding model with no external API — exclusively available inside Cursor IDE

stats.fact: Composer 2 scores 61.7 on Terminal-Bench 2.0, beating Claude Opus 4.6's 58.0 but trailing GPT-5.4's 75.1
Composer 2 achieves 73.7 on SWE-bench Multilingual (up from 65.9 in v1.5) and 61.3 on CursorBench (up from 44.2)
38% improvement on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and 12% jump on SWE-bench Multilingual over Composer 1.5
Pricing: Standard $0.50/M input and $2.50/M output tokens; Fast $1.50/M input and $7.50/M output tokens
Cursor valued at $29.3 billion; same day Bloomberg reported $50B valuation talks
Cursor has 1M+ daily active users and 50,000+ enterprise customers including Stripe, Figma, Salesforce, NVIDIA
92% of US developers use AI coding tools daily, 46% of all new code is AI-generated
45% of AI-generated code samples contain security vulnerabilities; 72% failure rate for AI-generated Java
Claude Code most-loved at 46% (DataCamp survey), Cursor second at 19%
Composer 2 built on Kimi K2.5 (Moonshot AI open-source) with ~25% from K2.5 architecture, rest from Cursor's pretraining+RL
Compaction-in-the-loop RL reduces context compaction errors by 50%, compresses 5000+ tokens to ~1000
Three Composer generations in 5 months: v1 (Oct 2025), v1.5 (Feb 2026), v2 (Mar 2026)
Cache-read pricing: $0.20/M tokens (Standard), $0.35/M tokens (Fast) vs $0.35/M for Composer 1.5

angles: IDE-native frontier model: Why Composer 2 matters because it's built specifically for the Cursor workflow, not as a general-purpose API model
Cost revolution: 86% price drop from v1.5 to v2 while being significantly more capable — frontier-level coding at $0.50/M input
Kimi K2.5 transparency controversy: The open-source base model question and what it means for trust in AI tool companies
Long-horizon agentic coding: Self-summarization and compaction-in-the-loop RL enabling hundreds of sequential actions without context loss
Composer vs Claude Code vs Copilot vs Codex: The four-way 2026 AI coding showdown and when each tool is the right choice
Speed vs quality tradeoff: Standard vs Fast variants and how to choose the right tier for your workflow
Enterprise readiness: SOC 2, SAML, SCIM compliance at $20/mo Pro — comparing enterprise features across AI coding tools
outline: Introduction: Cursor Composer 2 — A Frontier Model Built for the IDE
What Is Cursor Composer 2? (First in-house model, Kimi K2.5 base, 200K context, agentic focus)
Benchmark Deep Dive: Composer 2 vs Claude Opus 4.6 vs GPT-5.4 (Terminal-Bench 2.0, SWE-bench, CursorBench scores)
Architecture & Training: Continued Pretraining + Compaction-in-Loop RL (MoE architecture, self-summarization, 50% error reduction)
Pricing Breakdown: Standard vs Fast Variants & Plan Tiers ($0.50/$2.50, $1.50/$7.50, 86% cheaper than v1.5)
Composer 2 vs Composer 1.5: What Changed (38.7% CursorBench gain, 28.8% Terminal-Bench gain, pricing drop)
Competitor Comparison: Composer 2 vs Claude Code vs Copilot vs OpenAI Codex (feature matrix, pricing, best-for guidance)
The Kimi K2.5 Question: Transparency, Open Source, and Model Provenance (25% base, disclosure controversy)
Long-Horizon Agentic Coding: Self-Summarization and Multi-Step Workflows (hundreds of actions, compaction, worktrees)
Cursor Plan Guide: Hobby, Pro, Pro+, Ultra, Teams, Enterprise (which plan for which developer)
Getting Started with Composer 2 (setup, switching from Auto mode, best practices)
Known Limitations and Honest Assessment (no external API, GPT-5.4 leads benchmarks, vibe coding risks)
Security, Enterprise, and Compliance (SOC 2, SAML, SCIM, privacy mode, audit logs)
The Vibe Coding Debate: Power vs Responsibility (45% vulnerability rate, autonomous agent guardrails)
Future Outlook: Where Composer Goes Next (Glass alpha, self-driving codebases, MoE inference advances)
Conclusion: Who Should Use Composer 2 in 2026 (developer profiles, best use cases, when to choose alternatives)
